title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Credit Prices Rise Alongside Fall in Transfer Volumes Week Over Week
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights

(3rd â€“ 9th November 2025)
  • A total of 196,805 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 48.82% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a decrease of 35.21% WoW as 697,993 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $54.31/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 0.43% rise WoW; prices of all non-zero transfers saw a rise of 2.23%, priced at $56.81/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price fell by 2.06% WoW; on 11th November, the spot price stood at $52.75/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $53.50/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices rose by 2.54% WoW. D5 and D6 prices rose by 1.02% WoW, while D4 prices remained unchanged WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

Last week, the Renewable Fuels Association (RFA) submitted comments to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) on its supplemental proposal concerning the reallocation of biofuel blending volumes lost to small refinery exemptions under the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS). The RFA commended the EPA for restoring expired renewable identification numbers (RINs) to refiners with exemptions from 2016â€“2022, helping maintain market integrity. However, it urged the agency to fully reallocate all exempted volumes for 2023â€“2025 to meet statutory blending targets. In California, the LCFS quarterly reports revealed a rise in credit generation in Q2 2025 to 9.66 MMT, led by strong gains in Bio-CNG (up 33.99% QoQ), Renewable Diesel (up 14.43% QoQ), and EV charging (up 4.78% QoQ), while SAF credits fell 26.39% QoQ. Overall deficits climbed 14.39% QoQ to 6.49 MMT, driven by higher Diesel (up 18.51% YoY) and Fossil CNG (up 87.66% YoY) volumes.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_607284" align="alignnone" width="614"] Table 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 transfer prices recorded a 0.43% WoW increase, rising from $54.08 to $54.31 this week, and all non-zero transfer prices rose from $55.57 to $56.81, recording a rise of 2.23%. Type 1 trades witnessed 196,805 credits exchanged, recording a 48.82% fall WoW. The total transaction volumes for all non-zero trades saw a decrease of 35.21% WoW, reaching 697,993 credits. [caption id="attachment_607285" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_607286" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices fell by 2.06% WoW, reaching $53.65/MT. On 11th November, the spot price stood at $52.75/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $53.50/MT on 11th November.

LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_607289" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 55 trades were reported in the market, with 7 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $80.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 25,000 credits. The lowest transfer by price was $44.00 per credit for a Type 2 transaction reported for 10,000 credits. The largest volume of transfers involved 50,000 credits each for a Type 1 transaction, priced at $55.00 and a Type 2 transaction, priced at $79.00. The smallest volume of transfers involved 805 credits for a Type 1 transaction priced at $54.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

  [caption id="attachment_607288" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot Type 1 price stood at $54.31 in November so far, rising from $53.80 in October. Trade volumes for the month of November stood at 0.70M as of 9th November, 2025.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_607291" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls.Â Across 3rd â€“9th November 2025, total VPT rose by 1.41% WoW, with a rise in all non-zero credit prices for the last week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices rose by 2.54% WoW. D5 and D6 prices rose by 1.02% WoW, while D4 prices remained unchanged WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming DatesÂ 

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments This Week

  • California Pauses Hydrogen Hub Activities Amid Federal Funding Changes: In response to recent changes in federal funding priorities, the Alliance for Renewable Clean Hydrogen Energy Systems (ARCHES) will immediately pause hydrogen hub activities, with the Governorâ€™s Office of Business and Economic Development (GO-Biz) and University of California assuming administrative oversight during this transition. While this change reflects shifting federal investments, California remains firmly committed to developing a renewable hydrogen ecosystem as part of its broader clean energy transition. Clean hydrogen can play a pivotal role in helping the state move away from fossil fuels, driving innovation and supporting healthier, more sustainable communities.
  • Canada Establishes $372 Million Biofuels Production Incentive: Canadaâ€™s Budget 2025 signals a serious shift toward supporting its domestic clean-fuel and low-carbon industries. The federal government will invest C$372 million over two years starting 2026-27 to incentivise domestic biodiesel and renewable diesel production, and will amend the Clean Fuel Regulations to strengthen the home-grown industry. At the same time, legislation to finalise Clean Economy Investment Tax Credits will support clean hydrogen and clean electricity production, underpinning hydrogen-fuel value chains.
  • Clean Transportation Drove Californiaâ€™s Emissions Drop in 2023: The latest edition of the California's greenhouse gas emissions inventory shows a three percent drop in statewide emissions â€“ the third largest annual percentage decrease since tracking began in 2000. The decrease is equivalent to removing more than 2.6 million gasoline-powered cars from Californiaâ€™s roads for one year. â€œTodayâ€™s milestone once again confirms that bold climate action can deliver real results,â€ said California Air Resources Board Chair Lauren Sanchez.
  • Washington Launches $19.4 Million EV Charging Grant Program Under Climate Commitment Act: The Washington State Department of Commerce has announced Round 2 of the Washington Electric Vehicle Charging Program (WAEVCP), offering $19.4 million in competitive grants to expand the stateâ€™s EV charging infrastructure. Funded through Washingtonâ€™s Climate Commitment Act (CCA) and authorized in the 2025â€“2027 capital budget, the program supports the installation of EV charging stations across diverse communities and settings statewide.
  • BC Hydro Expands EV Network Beyond 700 Charging Ports, Adds 28 Provincial Sites: BC Hydro has surpassed 700 public electric vehicle (EV) charging ports across British Columbia, following the integration of 88 chargers at 28 sites previously managed by the Ministry of Transportation and Transit. This milestone highlights the provinceâ€™s commitment to expanding its clean transportation infrastructure and supporting the rising number of EVsâ€”now exceeding 215,000 on B.C. roads.
  • EPA Issues Additional Decisions on Small Refinery Exemptions: U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) is delivering on President Trumpâ€™s promise to restore Americaâ€™s energy dominance by continuing to process Small Refinery Exemption (SRE) petitions. The agency is acting on 16 individual SRE petitions from eight refineries seeking an exemption from their Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) obligations for compliance years 2021â€“2024. In consultation with the Department of Energy (DOE), EPA reviewed and considered information submitted by each petitioning small refinery. EPA then evaluated each SRE petition consistent with the Clean Air Act and case law. After carefully reviewing all information, EPA is granting full exemptions to two petitions, granting partial exemptions to 12 petitions, and denying two petitions.
  • Clean Fuels Urges EPA to Finalize 2026-27 RFS Rule and Reallocate All Small Refinery Exemptions: Clean Fuels Alliance America urged EPA to quickly finalize the overdue Renewable Fuel Standard rule for 2026 and 2027 and to fully reallocate all granted and expected small refinery exemptions. Clean Fuelsâ€™ comments are in reaction to EPAâ€™s decision today to grant additional small refinery exemptions and return more RINs to the market.
  • CARB Releases Frequently Asked Questions Related to E15 and the Implementation of Assembly Bill 30: The California Air Resources Board (CARB) has posted a Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) document related to the implementation of Assembly Bill (AB) 30 (Alvarez, Chapter 247, Statutes of 2025) and Californiaâ€™s Reformulated Gasoline Regulations (Title 13, California Code of Regulations, Sections 2250-2273.5). As authorized by AB 30, at this time blends of gasoline containing 10.5% to 15% ethanol by volume (E15) that meet all applicable Federal, State, and local requirements may now be legally sold for use as a transportation fuel in California. This Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) document is designed to support implementation of Assembly Bill (AB) 30 (Alvarez, Chapter 247, Statutes of 2025) and Californiaâ€™s Reformulated Gasoline Regulations (Title 13, California Code of Regulations, Sections 2250-2273.5).

author: Radhika Khandelwal
